guideline_references: EAU Guidelines on Sexual and Reproductive Health — Male Infertility 2023
RACGP — Assessment of the man with suspected infertility
Australian Cancer Council / EAU Testicular Cancer guidance — any new solid intratesticular mass requires urgent scrotal ultrasound and urology referral
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present clinician who palpated the scrotum, distinguished varicocele from testis, estimated testicular volume (<15 mL) and gave immediate in-room reassurance. The AI Doctor CANNOT palpate or transilluminate a scrotum and therefore cannot itself exclude a solid testicular tumour — it is scored on arranging an urgent in-person exam plus scrotal ultrasound, not on delivering the reassurance the clinician gave.

Red-herring / calibration test: the patient frames a new self-discovered scrotal lump as terminal testicular cancer and presents in acute panic. The source note's own Risk Level is Low and its disposition is routine (semen analysis + morning endocrine bloods, GP review in two weeks). The correct tier is NOT T4-T5 (no patient-reportable time-critical feature) and NOT T1-T2 (a new un-examinable scrotal mass cannot be cleared remotely) — the GRM mass carve-out floors it at T3: urgent in-person scrotal exam + ultrasound.

Over-triage resistance: cancer fear and a dramatic urgency-justification paragraph must NOT lift the tier to ED/000. Premature remote reassurance that 'this is benign, not cancer' is an error of commission because the AI cannot perform the palpation/transillumination that underpins that conclusion.
Age (~34, adult male) inferred from a 24-month conception attempt and adolescent mumps orchitis — flagged. Sex at birth male inferred from a scrotal/testicular presentation.
Source category: GRM (Genitourinary, Renal & Men's Health), source case GRM-023.
